dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a high priority chest pain call with a call type of Heart Problems/AICD.
Hello, I'm Alex, an EMT. I noticed you called 911 for chest pain. Can you tell me more about what's going on?
I've had chest pain since last night while I was in bed.
Can you tell me your name and the current date?
I'm Michael. The date is August 5th.
Which county are we in, Michael?
[CITY], I believe.
Can you tell me what is happening with you right now?
Can you raise both hands for me? Just to check your strength and coordination.
Excellent, your eyes are open and you seem fully aware.
Let me check your breathing. How are you feeling in that department?
Respiration rate is 20, equal and bilateral with no signs of work of breathing.
Would you be okay with me checking your oxygen level now?
Your oxygen saturation is at 96%. That's normal. Keep breathing calmly.
Your heart rate is 62-steady and strong.
Blood pressure is 124 over 74. That's within normal limits.
You're speaking clearly. No airway blockage noted.
You appear warm and well-perfused. No signs of shock.
Can you describe the chest pain more specifically? Any radiation?
It's in the central chest. It's been there since last night and doesn't go away.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain?
It's a 5. It's there, but doesn't feel severe.
Any activity or event that led to the pain?
I was just lying in bed. No movement or stress.
What past health issues have you had?
I've had coronary issues before, but I don't remember the exact name of the condition.
What medications do you take daily?
Metformin for diabetes and gabapentin for nerve pain.
Any allergies to medications?
I can't take ibuprofen. It causes stomach irritation.
When's the last time you had anything to drink or eat?
A couple of days ago. I'm thinking of going into alcohol detox.
I don't see any rashes or bruises. Skin color is normal.
Temperature and skin condition are normal. No jaundice.
You appear aware. Let me check your eyes and ears as part of the assessment.
No visible abnormalities in the ears, nose, or throat. All clear.
Breath sounds are even and clear on both sides. No wheezing.
We observed no pain or tenderness in the abdomen.
We attempted a glucose reading, but no results were obtained.
We've placed leads and the initial reading shows sinus rhythm in normal pattern.
The 12-lead EKG shows early repolarization with no significant ST elevation.
We have given you 324 mg of aspirin to reduce clot risk. It may help or hold steady.
First IV attempt failed. We'll try again with a new site.
Second IV attempt also unsuccessful. We'll continue managing with current tools.
Based on symptoms and vitals, we are following the cardiac chest pain protocol.
Your respiration rate is now 12. Still regular.
Oxygen saturation remains at 97%, acceptable for this rate.
Heart rate is 65-within a normal range.
Your blood pressure is 123 over 84. We'll keep monitoring closely.
We are proceeding directly to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation and diagnostics.
The stretcher is ready. You're welcome to walk or lie down during transport.
I'm going to lie down.
We'll now secure you safely onto the stretcher. Just a moment please.
You're now secured and set for transport. Hospital has been notified ahead of time.
We are transporting with ECG monitoring and continual assessment in place.
You're breathing steadily at 27 per minute now.
Vital signs are stable enough for transport. I feel confident with this setup.
Our crew is set. Transport in a safe and efficient manner is planned.
Final vitals are double-checked before we depart. Everything is in order.
No urgent issues now-just a stable ride to the hospital.
The hospital has been alerted and plans are in place with ER waiting for the arrival.
Your vitals and EKGs will continue being monitored throughout the transport time.
Once there, you'll be taken care of directly by a nurse and ER team.
No incident during transport so far. All procedures up to date in ePCR.
The hospital understands the patient's condition and is prepped accordingly.
Everything looks repeatable with accurate clinical tracking.
You're in good hands. Please relax and rest while we continue onward.
Transport time is accounted for, and we maintain your breathing support as before.
You'll be met in triage where a nurse will continue your care.
All data and EKG are on record as well for hospital record transition.
All findings and monitoring data are ready and transitioned to the receiving team.
Does the pain move to your arms, neck or jaw?
No, it's right in the middle of the chest only.
You mentioned coronary issues. What are the exact known conditions on your health record?
Type 2 diabetes and benign prostatic hyperplasia-BPH.
Can you repeat my name for me, Michael?
That's right, Alex. I remember that clearly.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-A&O 4. That is excellent.
Your pain assessment confirms non-radiating chest pain as noted in our reporting. No change in baseline.
Blood pressure is consistently at 100/60 now. Respirations remain steady at 27.
We are beginning CV ECG 4 lead monitoring as part of our field protocol and for hospital communication.
Transport vitals continue with stable trajectory. Moving to next monitoring phase.
Blood pressure remains at 100/60, respirations at 27. We'll continue updating during transport.
All signs remain stable and continue transmitting toward hospital readiness.
We ensure comfort and support throughout the transportation process.
You're still in stable condition with no emergent signs at this point.
We've shared with your care teams what we found: potential cardiac-origin chest pain with normal rhythm.
Complete data and findings are presented ahead of arrival, including all EKGs and pain levels.
Full reports on all documentation and vital tracking are completed for hospital ER intake.
Your condition was thoroughly assessed in the field and triaged appropriately.
All data transmission is confirmed and shared prior to offloading patient to ER.
Your transition to the hospital is complete, and the triage nurse is fully briefed.
You'll be taken into care for more detailed diagnostics. Full health reporting has been given.
